badge.label: Technical Basics
badge.description: Passed the Technical Academy foundation quiz.

sections.label: 01 Intro
sections.title: 01 Getting to Know Technical Analysis
sections.completion_signal: You can explain that TA studies price and volume, then uses probability rather than prediction.

pages.heading: What is technical analysis?
pages.body: Technical analysis evaluates securities by analyzing statistical data generated by market activity, primarily price and volume. Unlike fundamental analysis (which reads financial statements), TA focuses on chart patterns and price behavior to anticipate future price direction.

pages.heading: TA vs Fundamental: how they work together
pages.body: Fundamental analysis tells you whether a company is worth buying. Technical analysis tells you when to buy it. It is wise to combine both, use TA for execution discipline. Never use TA alone without understanding what the business does.

pages.heading: IDX context
pages.body: IDX (Bursa Efek Indonesia) has over 900 listed companies and a rapidly growing retail investor base. Short-term price moves are heavily sentiment-driven, making TA especially relevant. The most significant price moves tend to occur in the first and last 30 minutes of each trading session. IDX trading hours: pre-opening 08:45–09:00 WIB, session I 09:00–12:00, session II 13:30–16:00.

pages.heading: Common Mistake
pages.body: Do not add indicators before reading price action. Indicators can confirm a view, but they should not replace structure.

checks.question: Which data does Technical Analysis read most directly?

options.label: Price and volume
options.feedback: Right. TA begins with price behavior and participation.

options.label: Director biography
options.feedback: That can matter for fundamentals, but it is not the main TA input.

options.label: Dividend policy only
options.feedback: Dividends can affect a thesis, but TA is built from price and volume.

checks.question: What is the healthiest way to use TA?

options.label: Predict exact future prices
options.feedback: TA should not be treated as certainty.

options.label: Frame probabilities and risk
options.feedback: Correct. The point is better decisions under uncertainty.

options.label: Ignore invalidation if the pattern looks nice
options.feedback: A nice pattern without invalidation is still a risky guess.

content_blocks.heading: What is technical analysis?
content_blocks.body: Technical analysis evaluates securities by analyzing statistical data generated by market activity, primarily price and volume. Unlike fundamental analysis (which reads financial statements), TA focuses on chart patterns and price behavior to anticipate future price direction.

content_blocks.heading: TA vs Fundamental: how they work together
content_blocks.body: Fundamental analysis tells you whether a company is worth buying. Technical analysis tells you when to buy it. It is wise to combine both, use TA for execution discipline. Never use TA alone without understanding what the business does.

content_blocks.heading: IDX context
content_blocks.body: IDX (Bursa Efek Indonesia) has over 900 listed companies and a rapidly growing retail investor base. Short-term price moves are heavily sentiment-driven, making TA especially relevant. The most significant price moves tend to occur in the first and last 30 minutes of each trading session. IDX trading hours: pre-opening 08:45–09:00 WIB, session I 09:00–12:00, session II 13:30–16:00.

content_blocks.heading: Common Mistake
content_blocks.body: Do not add indicators before reading price action. Indicators can confirm a view, but they should not replace structure.

content_blocks.title: Key Insights
bullets: Indicators confirm that price action decides. Use MA, MACD, RSI, and volume to support your reading of the chart, not to replace it.

sections.label: 02 Market Structure
sections.title: Market Structure
sections.completion_signal: You can identify whether a chart is trending, ranging, broken, or worth deeper analysis.

pages.heading: Concept
pages.body: Structure is the market's skeleton. Bullish structure often makes higher highs and higher lows. Bearish structure often makes lower highs and lower lows. Sideways structure moves inside a range.

pages.heading: Chart Cue
pages.body: Mark support and resistance as areas, not exact magic lines. Look for bases, trendline breaks, accumulation, distribution, and whether the bigger timeframe agrees.

pages.heading: How To Read It
pages.body: Begin from the bigger timeframe. If daily structure is healthy, a lower timeframe can help with precision. If daily structure is broken, a lower timeframe bounce may still be only noise.

pages.heading: Common Mistake
pages.body: Avoid calling every dip a support and every green candle a breakout. Structure needs location, follow-through, and volume context.

checks.question: A chart keeps printing higher highs and higher lows. What structure is this?

options.label: Uptrend
options.feedback: Correct. Higher highs and higher lows are classic bullish structure.

options.label: Downtrend
options.feedback: Downtrends usually print lower highs and lower lows.

options.label: No structure can be read
options.feedback: Structure can often be read from swing behavior.

checks.question: Why mark support and resistance as areas?

options.label: Because the exact line always holds
options.feedback: Exact lines are fragile; markets often test zones.

options.label: Because price often reacts around zones
options.feedback: Right. Areas reduce false precision.

options.label: So invalidation is never needed
options.feedback: You still need invalidation.

content_blocks.heading: Concept
content_blocks.body: Structure is the market's skeleton. Bullish structure often makes higher highs and higher lows. Bearish structure often makes lower highs and lower lows. Sideways structure moves inside a range.

content_blocks.heading: Chart Cue
content_blocks.body: Mark support and resistance as areas, not exact magic lines. Look for bases, trendline breaks, accumulation, distribution, and whether the bigger timeframe agrees.

content_blocks.heading: How To Read It
content_blocks.body: Begin from the bigger timeframe. If daily structure is healthy, a lower timeframe can help with precision. If daily structure is broken, a lower timeframe bounce may still be only noise.

content_blocks.heading: Common Mistake
content_blocks.body: Avoid calling every dip a support and every green candle a breakout. Structure needs location, follow-through, and volume context.

sections.label: 03 Candlesticks
sections.title: Candlesticks
sections.completion_signal: You can read OHLC, body, wick, timeframe context, and confirmation before acting on candles.

pages.heading: Concept
pages.body: A candle compresses open, high, low, and close into one visual. The body shows the open-close fight. The wick shows rejected prices.

pages.heading: Chart Cue
pages.body: Single-candle patterns include doji variants, shooting star, hammer, inverted hammer, marubozu, and spinning top. Multi-candle patterns include engulfing, harami, morning star, evening star, three white soldiers, and three black crows.

pages.heading: How To Read It
pages.body: A candle pattern matters more at a key area than in the middle of nowhere. Confirmation can come from the next candle, structure, volume, or a broader setup.

pages.heading: Common Mistake
pages.body: Do not buy every hammer or sell every doji. A candle is a clue, not a full trade plan.

pattern_cards.label: Hammer
pattern_cards.cue: Long lower wick near support; watch for follow-through.

pattern_cards.label: Shooting Star
pattern_cards.cue: Long upper wick near resistance; watch for rejection.

pattern_cards.label: Engulfing
pattern_cards.cue: Second candle overwhelms prior body; context decides meaning.

pattern_cards.label: Doji
pattern_cards.cue: Indecision; strongest when it appears at a meaningful area.

checks.question: A long upper wick near resistance usually suggests what?

options.label: Buying was rejected at higher prices
options.feedback: Correct. The wick shows a failed push higher.

options.label: A guaranteed breakout tomorrow
options.feedback: No single candle guarantees a breakout.

options.label: Context no longer matters
options.feedback: Context still matters most.

checks.question: What makes a candle pattern safer to act on?

options.label: A bigger chart label
options.feedback: Labels do not create edge.

options.label: Confirmation from area, volume, or follow-through
options.feedback: Right. Candles need confirmation.

options.label: Entering before the candle closes
options.feedback: Early entries carry extra fakeout risk.

content_blocks.heading: Concept
content_blocks.body: A candle compresses open, high, low, and close into one visual. The body shows the open-close fight. The wick shows rejected prices.

content_blocks.heading: Chart Cue
content_blocks.body: Single-candle patterns include doji variants, shooting star, hammer, inverted hammer, marubozu, and spinning top. Multi-candle patterns include engulfing, harami, morning star, evening star, three white soldiers, and three black crows.

content_blocks.heading: How To Read It
content_blocks.body: A candle pattern matters more at a key area than in the middle of nowhere. Confirmation can come from the next candle, structure, volume, or a broader setup.

content_blocks.heading: Common Mistake
content_blocks.body: Do not buy every hammer or sell every doji. A candle is a clue, not a full trade plan.

sections.label: 04 Patterns
sections.title: Patterns
sections.completion_signal: You can validate a pattern by structure, location, volume, and invalidation.

pages.heading: Concept
pages.body: Patterns are repeated structures that show how supply and demand are changing. Useful patterns include range or box, VCP, double bottom, cup and handle, head and shoulders, and ascending triangle.

pages.heading: Chart Cue
pages.body: A valid setup should be visible in structure, appear in a sensible location, show volume behavior that supports the story, and have a clear invalidation point.

pages.heading: How To Read It
pages.body: For a breakout, look for tightening volatility, pressure near resistance, and volume expansion. For reversal patterns, look for failed breakdowns, higher lows, or clear rejection.

pages.heading: Common Mistake
pages.body: Do not force a famous pattern onto messy price action. If the setup is unclear, no trade is also a decision.

pattern_cards.label: VCP
pattern_cards.cue: Swings tighten as selling pressure dries up.

pattern_cards.label: Double Bottom
pattern_cards.cue: Second low holds; confirmation comes through neckline strength.

pattern_cards.label: Cup and Handle
pattern_cards.cue: Rounded recovery followed by a smaller consolidation.

pattern_cards.label: Head and Shoulders
pattern_cards.cue: Trend weakens through lower right shoulder and neckline break.

checks.question: Which set best validates a chart pattern?

options.label: A famous pattern name only
options.feedback: The name alone is not enough.

options.label: Structure, location, volume, and invalidation
options.feedback: Correct. That is the checklist.

options.label: One green candle anywhere
options.feedback: A single candle without context is weak.

checks.question: What should you do when the pattern is unclear?

options.label: Force the closest pattern name
options.feedback: Forcing patterns creates false confidence.

options.label: Wait or skip until structure is clearer
options.feedback: Good. No trade can be the cleanest trade.

options.label: Buy more so the pattern works
options.feedback: Position size does not fix an unclear setup.

content_blocks.heading: Concept
content_blocks.body: Patterns are repeated structures that show how supply and demand are changing. Useful patterns include range or box, VCP, double bottom, cup and handle, head and shoulders, and ascending triangle.

content_blocks.heading: Chart Cue
content_blocks.body: A valid setup should be visible in structure, appear in a sensible location, show volume behavior that supports the story, and have a clear invalidation point.

content_blocks.heading: How To Read It
content_blocks.body: For a breakout, look for tightening volatility, pressure near resistance, and volume expansion. For reversal patterns, look for failed breakdowns, higher lows, or clear rejection.

content_blocks.heading: Common Mistake
content_blocks.body: Do not force a famous pattern onto messy price action. If the setup is unclear, no trade is also a decision.

sections.label: 05 Moving Average
sections.title: Moving Average
sections.completion_signal: You can use moving averages as context and confirmation, not as blind buy or sell buttons.

pages.heading: Concept
pages.body: Moving averages smooth price. SMA gives equal weight, EMA reacts faster, and WMA weights recent prices. All of them lag because they use past data.

pages.heading: Chart Cue
pages.body: Common periods include 5, 10, 20, 60, 100, and 200. Slope can show trend quality, while MA 200 often acts as a long-term context line.

pages.heading: How To Read It
pages.body: Use MA slope, dynamic support or resistance, and confluence with structure. Golden cross and death cross are context signals, not automatic orders.

pages.heading: Common Mistake
pages.body: Do not buy only because price touches an MA. Ask whether structure, volume, and risk still make sense.

pages.heading: MACD Note
pages.body: MACD can confirm momentum with crossovers, improving histogram, or divergence. It should support your read, not replace it.

checks.question: Why are moving averages called lagging indicators?

options.label: They are calculated from past prices
options.feedback: Correct. They smooth history, so they respond after price changes.

options.label: They know tomorrow's close
options.feedback: No indicator knows tomorrow.

options.label: They only use volume
options.feedback: Moving averages are price-based unless stated otherwise.

content_blocks.heading: Concept
content_blocks.body: Moving averages smooth price. SMA gives equal weight, EMA reacts faster, and WMA weights recent prices. All of them lag because they use past data.

content_blocks.heading: Chart Cue
content_blocks.body: Common periods include 5, 10, 20, 60, 100, and 200. Slope can show trend quality, while MA 200 often acts as a long-term context line.

content_blocks.heading: How To Read It
content_blocks.body: Use MA slope, dynamic support or resistance, and confluence with structure. Golden cross and death cross are context signals, not automatic orders.

content_blocks.heading: Common Mistake
content_blocks.body: Do not buy only because price touches an MA. Ask whether structure, volume, and risk still make sense.

content_blocks.heading: MACD Note
content_blocks.body: MACD can confirm momentum with crossovers, improving histogram, or divergence. It should support your read, not replace it.

sections.label: 06 Thinking Framework
sections.title: Thinking Framework
sections.completion_signal: You can run the 6-step technical routine without guessing.

pages.heading: Concept
pages.body: The routine is: start with context, mark key areas, identify the setup, add confirmation, build the trade plan, then execute and review.

pages.heading: Chart Cue
pages.body: For JPFA-style practice, read the daily context first, then use 1H structure for a cup-and-handle style precision entry. For SSIA-style practice, watch support near MA 200, doji confirmation, and MACD divergence.

pages.heading: How To Read It
pages.body: The framework keeps you from jumping straight to a signal. Every trade needs context, area, setup, confirmation, risk, and review.

pages.heading: Common Mistake
pages.body: Skipping the plan step turns analysis into impulse. Know entry, invalidation, target area, and position size before clicking buy.

checks.question: What is the first step in the 6-step framework?

options.label: Start with context
options.feedback: Correct. Big-picture context comes first.

options.label: Execute immediately
options.feedback: Execution comes after the plan.

options.label: Add every indicator
options.feedback: Indicators are confirmation tools, not the starting point.

content_blocks.heading: Concept
content_blocks.body: The routine is: start with context, mark key areas, identify the setup, add confirmation, build the trade plan, then execute and review.

content_blocks.heading: Chart Cue
content_blocks.body: For JPFA-style practice, read the daily context first, then use 1H structure for a cup-and-handle style precision entry. For SSIA-style practice, watch support near MA 200, doji confirmation, and MACD divergence.

content_blocks.heading: How To Read It
content_blocks.body: The framework keeps you from jumping straight to a signal. Every trade needs context, area, setup, confirmation, risk, and review.

content_blocks.heading: Common Mistake
content_blocks.body: Skipping the plan step turns analysis into impulse. Know entry, invalidation, target area, and position size before clicking buy.

sections.label: 07 Quiz
sections.title: Technical Basics Quiz
sections.completion_signal: Score 80 percent or better to earn Technical Basics.

sections.label: 08 Glossary
sections.title: Glossary
sections.completion_signal: Use this any time you need a quick definition.

quiz_questions.prompt: Technical Analysis most directly studies which pair?
quiz_questions.feedback_correct: Yes. Price and volume are the base inputs.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Review Intro: TA starts with price and volume.

quiz_questions.prompt: What mindset should a beginner use with TA?

options.label: TA removes the need for stops

quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: TA is a probability tool, not a crystal ball.

quiz_questions.prompt: What should be read before indicators?
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: The routine starts with price action and structure.

quiz_questions.prompt: Higher highs and higher lows usually describe what?
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Higher highs and higher lows point to bullish structure.

quiz_questions.prompt: Support and resistance should usually be marked as...
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Support and resistance behave more like areas than exact lines.

quiz_questions.prompt: Why start from the bigger timeframe?
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Bigger timeframe context keeps small moves in perspective.

quiz_questions.prompt: A candle body shows the relationship between...
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: The body is built from open and close.

quiz_questions.prompt: A long upper wick near resistance can suggest...
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: A long upper wick often shows rejection, especially near resistance.

quiz_questions.prompt: Which answer is a multi-candle pattern?
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Engulfing uses more than one candle.

quiz_questions.prompt: What should you do before acting on a candle signal?
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Candle clues need context and confirmation.

quiz_questions.prompt: A good pattern should include which validation set?
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: Pattern validation needs structure, location, volume, and invalidation.

options.label: Tightening volatility as selling pressure dries up

quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: VCP is about contractions and pressure drying up.

quiz_questions.prompt: What is the safest next action when a pattern is unclear?
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: If it is unclear, waiting is a valid decision.

quiz_questions.prompt: Put the first two framework steps in order.
quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: The framework begins with context, then key areas.

quiz_questions.prompt: Before execution, the trade plan should know...

options.label: Entry, invalidation, target area, and size

quiz_questions.feedback_wrong: A plan needs entry, invalidation, target, and position size.

categories.summary: Practice order execution, sizing, scaling, exits, and post-trade review.
categories.coming_soon_copy: Coming soon. This academy track will cover transaction planning, sizing, execution discipline, and review.

glossary.definition: A phase where stronger hands appear to absorb supply before a possible markup.
A price zone used for support or resistance instead of a single exact line.
A consolidation structure where price pauses and prepares for a possible next move.
A condition or view that favors lower prices.
A move below a support area that may signal weakness.
A move above a resistance area that may signal fresh demand.
A condition or view that favors higher prices.
The candle area between open and close.
A rounded recovery followed by a smaller consolidation before a possible breakout.
A bearish context signal when a shorter moving average crosses below a longer one.
A phase where supply appears to be sold into strength.
A disagreement between price movement and an indicator such as MACD.
A candle where open and close are close together, often signaling indecision.
A structure that often forms lower highs and lower lows.
Exponential moving average, a moving average that reacts more to recent prices.
A bullish context signal when a shorter moving average crosses above a longer one.
A candle with a long lower wick that can show rejection of lower prices near support.
A two-candle pattern where the second body sits inside the prior body.
A reversal structure with a head, two shoulders, and a neckline.
The price condition that tells you your trade idea is wrong.
Bearish swing behavior where each high and low forms below the previous one.
A momentum indicator often used for crossover, histogram, and divergence confirmation.
A 200-period moving average often used for long-term trend context.
A smoothed price line calculated from past prices.
A key confirmation area in patterns such as head and shoulders or double bottom.
Short-term movement that does not change the bigger structure.
A price area where supply may slow or reject upward movement.
The comparison between planned downside risk and possible upside reward.
The candle line that shows prices traded beyond the body and then rejected or left behind.
A range-bound structure without a clear uptrend or downtrend.
Simple moving average, a moving average that weights each period equally.
A price area where demand may slow or reject downward movement.
A line connecting swing points to visualize trend direction or breaks.
A structure that often forms higher highs and higher lows.
Volatility Contraction Pattern, where swings tighten as supply dries up.
The number of shares traded, used to judge participation.
Weighted moving average, a moving average that gives chosen periods more influence.
